programs.name: KanCare Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: If your parent has Medicare and monthly income under $1,801, Kansas will pay their Medicare premiums and possibly deductibles — saving up to $2,096/year.

localEntities.name: KDHE KanCare offices statewide; call 1-800-792-4884 for local

programs.intro: If your parent struggles to afford Medicare premiums, deductibles, or copays, Kansas's Medicare Savings Programs can eliminate these costs. The state pays Medicare Part B premiums ($174.70/month in 2024) for everyone who qualifies, plus deductibles and copays for those with the lowest incomes. Three programs serve different income levels: QMB covers the most costs for incomes up to $1,350/month, while SLMB and QI cover premiums only for incomes up to $1,801/month. All three programs automatically qualify your parent for Extra Help with prescription drug costs, potentially saving thousands more per year. Your parent must already have Medicare Part A to qualify, and asset limits apply ($9,950 for individuals in 2026).
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premiums ($2,096/year) plus potential Extra Help savings (up to $5,030/year)

exemptAssets: Life insurance up to $1,500 face value

otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at KanCare website or call 1-800-792-4884 — eligible the month you apply with possible retroactive coverage.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income (Social Security statements, pension), bank statements, Medicare card, and ID

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at kancare.ks.gov or call 1-800-792-4884 for paper application

steps.title: Await determination
steps.description: Coverage begins the month you apply if approved, with possible retroactive coverage for previous months

applicationGuide.processingTime: Coverage begins the month of application if approved
applicationGuide.tip: Income and asset limits change every May — call 1-800-792-4884 to confirm current limits before applying

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.description: Most comprehensive coverage for lowest incomes
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums, deductibles ($240 in 2024), coinsurance, and copayments

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums only

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.description: Premium help for highest qualifying incomes
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums only

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Life insurance with face value up to $1,500

contentSections.text: All three programs automatically qualify your parent for Extra Help with prescription drug costs — potentially saving up to $5,030 per year even if they only get premium help.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Kansas uses a $20 income disregard, meaning they subtract $20 from her monthly income when calculating eligibility. If she's still over, check if any income sources don't count (like certain veteran benefits) by calling 1-800-792-4884.

faqs.question: Can Dad keep his house and car if he qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes. The home where he lives (any value) and one car don't count toward the $9,950 asset limit. Neither do burial plots, personal belongings, or life insurance policies worth $1,500 or less.

faqs.question: Does this cover all of Mom's medical expenses?
faqs.answer: No. These programs only pay specific Medicare costs (premiums, deductibles, copays). They don't cover non-Medicare services like long-term care, dental, or vision. For broader Medicaid coverage, ask about other KanCare programs.

faqs.question: What happens if Dad's income changes after he's approved?
faqs.answer: He must report changes within 10 days to avoid overpayments. If his income drops, he might qualify for a higher tier of coverage. If it rises above limits, he'll lose coverage but keep Extra Help for the rest of the year.

faqs.question: Does Mom need to be on Medicare first before applying?
faqs.answer: Yes. She must already have Medicare Part A. For SLMB and QI programs, she also needs Medicare Part B. If she's not enrolled in Medicare yet, do that first through Social Security at 1-800-772-1213.

faqs.question: Will this affect any other benefits Dad receives?
faqs.answer: This won't reduce Social Security, pensions, or SNAP benefits. It actually helps — the automatic Extra Help for prescription drugs can save thousands, and providers can't balance bill him for Medicare-covered services.

programs.name: SNAP (Food Assistance)
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery money for aging parents on fixed incomes — no gross income limit if they're 60+.

localEntities.name: Local Kansas Department of Children and Families (DCF) offices

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggling with grocery costs, SNAP provides monthly benefits loaded on an EBT card. Most seniors qualify for $100-300/month, though the exact amount depends on income and household size. Because Kansas has no gross income limit for households where everyone is 60+ or disabled, many aging parents qualify even if they think they earn too much.

The program covers food purchases at authorized stores but not prepared meals or non-food items. Your parent will need to complete an application through their local Department of Children and Families (DCF) office, which can arrange phone or home interviews for seniors who can't visit in person.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly benefits of $100-300 estimated from maximum benefit amounts

summary: Net income below poverty level after deductions
otherRequirements: Must live together and share food purchases with other household members

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online, by phone, or in person at your local DCF office — phone interviews available for seniors.

steps.title: Contact your local DCF office
steps.description: Call or visit to start your application. They can arrange a phone interview if you can't visit in person.

steps.title: Complete the Kansas Food Assistance application
steps.description: Provide information about income, expenses, and household members who share food.

steps.title: Attend your eligibility interview
steps.description: DCF will schedule a phone or in-person interview to review your application and documents.

steps.title: Receive your EBT card
steps.description: If approved, benefits are loaded monthly on an Electronic Benefits Transfer card for grocery purchases.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Typically 30 days, expedited processing available for urgent need
applicationGuide.tip: Emphasize medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs — these deductions often make the difference for seniors who think they earn too much.

contentSections.heading: Gross Income Limits by Household Size (Under 60)
contentSections.footnote: October 2025 - September 2026. No gross income limit for households where everyone is 60+ or disabled.

categories.name: Identity
items: Proof of Kansas residency (utility bill, lease)

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of any other income (pensions, VA benefits)

contentSections.text: Many seniors who receive SSI are automatically eligible for SNAP. Even if your parent thinks their income is too high, households where everyone is 60+ have no gross income limit in Kansas — only net income matters after deductions.

faqs.question: My mom gets Social Security — does that make her ineligible?
faqs.answer: No. Social Security counts as income, but households where everyone is 60+ have no gross income limit in Kansas. Her net income after deductions for medical expenses and shelter costs must be at or below the poverty level. Many seniors qualify even with Social Security.

faqs.question: Can she use SNAP benefits for prepared meals or restaurant food?
faqs.answer: Generally no. SNAP benefits work like a debit card for groceries at authorized stores, but cannot be used for hot prepared foods, alcohol, tobacco, or non-food items. Some exceptions exist for seniors in certain meal programs.

faqs.question: What if Dad lives with my sister but they don't share groceries?
faqs.answer: If they buy and prepare food separately, they count as separate households for SNAP. But if they share meals or grocery shopping, they must apply together as one household, which could affect eligibility and benefit amounts.

faqs.question: My parents own their home — will that disqualify them?
faqs.answer: No. The primary home doesn't count toward asset limits regardless of value. Kansas also has no asset limits for households where everyone is 60+ or disabled, so bank accounts and savings generally won't disqualify seniors.

faqs.question: How much will my parent actually receive in benefits?
faqs.answer: It depends on income, deductions, and household size. Maximum is about $291 for one person, $535 for two people, but most seniors get less. Medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs increase the benefit amount.

faqs.question: What counts as medical expenses for the deduction?
faqs.answer: Any unreimbursed medical costs over $35/month for seniors 60+: Medicare premiums, prescription drugs, doctor visits, medical supplies, and transportation to medical appointments. Keep all receipts — this deduction often makes seniors eligible.

programs.name: LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: One-time payment toward your parent's heating or cooling bills if they're on a fixed income.

localEntities.name: Local DCF Service Centers; Evergy Wichita Connect for events

programs.intro:
If your parent's monthly income is below $1,956 for a single person ($2,644 for a couple), they may qualify for a one-time payment toward their heating or cooling bills through Kansas LIHEAP. The payment amount depends on their income, household size, type of home, and fuel type — but it goes directly to their utility company or fuel vendor to reduce what they owe.

This is seasonal assistance with limited funding. Applications typically open January through March each year, and once funding runs out, the program closes until the next year. Your parent must be personally responsible for paying their heating costs — if heat is fully included in subsidized rent, they won't qualify.
programs.savingsSource: Amount varies by household income, size, dwelling type, and fuel costs

summary: Combined household income below 150% federal poverty level
Must pay heating costs directly (not included in rent)
U.S. citizen or qualified alien in household
otherRequirements: Must be personally responsible for heating costs
One application per household per program year
Cannot receive both state and tribal LIHEAP benefits in same year
College students must live at home full-time to be counted in household

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through the DCF portal or at local offices during the application period (typically January-March).

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect income proof for all household members, recent fuel bills, and rental agreement if applicable.

steps.title: Complete LIHEAP-specific application
steps.description: This is separate from other benefit applications and includes heating/cooling details. All adults in the household must sign.

steps.title: Submit during application period
steps.description: Applications are only accepted during designated periods (typically January 20 - March 31). Submit online, by mail, or in-person.

steps.title: Payment goes directly to utility
steps.description: If approved, the payment goes directly to your parent's utility company or fuel vendor, not to them personally.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing begins after submission during the application period
applicationGuide.waitlist: No waitlist - once federal funding is exhausted, applications close until next year
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the application period. This is first-come, first-served until funding runs out.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (2026)
contentSections.footnote: For households larger than 8, add $688 per additional person. Based on 150% of federal poverty level.

categories.name: Utility Information
items: Rental/lease agreement if heat included in rent

categories.name: Other Documents
items: Proof of citizenship/legal status if not receiving other assistance

contentSections.text: Applications are only accepted during limited periods (typically January-March) and funding runs out each year. Apply as early as possible once applications open.
Everyone living at the address counts toward the income limit, even adult children or other relatives not applying for benefits. College students living in dorms don't count as household members.

faqs.question: What if my parent's heat is included in their rent?
faqs.answer: If heat is fully included in subsidized rent, they won't qualify. But if they pay rent plus utilities separately, or pay the landlord directly for heating costs, they may still be eligible.

faqs.question: Does my parent's savings account count against the income limit?
faqs.answer: No, Kansas LIHEAP has no asset limits. Only monthly income matters for eligibility, not savings or property value.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if I live with them and work full-time?
faqs.answer: Your income would count toward the household total since you live there. The combined income of everyone in the home must be below the limit for your household size.

faqs.question: What if my parent already gets help from the tribal energy program?
faqs.answer: They cannot receive both state LIHEAP and tribal LIHEAP benefits in the same year. Tribal members are typically referred to their tribe's program instead.

faqs.question: How much money will my parent actually receive?
faqs.answer: The payment amount varies based on income level, household size, type of dwelling, heating fuel type, and current utility rates. The payment goes directly to the utility company, not to your parent.

faqs.question: What if we miss the application deadline?
faqs.answer: You'll have to wait until the next program year. Applications are only accepted during designated periods, typically January through March, and the program closes once funding is exhausted.
